prompt: A highly detailed 19th-century naturalist scientific illustration depicting the concept of gradient descent as a topographical terrain study. A mountain landscape is shown with contour lines, where a path descends from an initial high error point at the peak down to a global minimum in the valley. Along the path, clearly labeled points show Iteration 1, Iteration 2, and Iteration 3, with arrows indicating steepest descent and decreasing error. Include annotations such as gradient vector, descent path, convergence nearing, and global minimum error. Rendered in fine ink linework with precise cross-hatching shading, handwritten scientific annotations, marginal notes, and diagrammatic labels. Composition resembles Darwin-era geological or botanical scientific plates from the 1800s. Use sepia tones, aged parchment texture, and symmetrical plate layout with decorative naturalist borders. Strictly no modern elements, no digital rendering, no photorealism.
tradition_approach: Fine ink linework, cross-hatching, handwritten annotations, scientific labeling, contour mapping, and parchment texture to replicate Darwin-era observational scientific plates.
